# Imitation Learning

## Minimal Setup

Imitation learning aims to learn a policy from expert demonstrations:

$$
\pi_\theta(a_t \mid o_t)
$$

where $o_t$ is the robot observation and $a_t$ is the expert action.

---

## Behavioral Cloning

Behavioral cloning casts imitation learning as supervised learning:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{BC}(\theta)
=
\mathbb{E}_{(o_t,a_t)\sim\mathcal{D}}
\left[
\lVert \pi_\theta(o_t) - a_t \rVert^2
\right]
$$

TODO: Insert diagram of supervised policy learning at `figures/behavioral_cloning_pipeline.png`.

---

## Why Not Only Reinforcement Learning?

- Reward design is difficult for contact-rich tasks.
- Exploration can be unsafe or slow on hardware.
- Resetting physical systems is expensive.
- Demonstrations provide useful structure before trial-and-error optimization.

---

## Covariate Shift

- During training, the policy sees expert states.
- During deployment, policy errors can push the robot into unseen states.
- Small mistakes can compound over time.

TODO: Insert covariate shift trajectory illustration at `figures/covariate_shift.png`.

---

## Multimodal Actions

For the same observation, there may be several valid expert actions:

- grasp from the left or the right
- push or pull
- wait, retreat, or continue contact

Mean-squared behavioral cloning can average modes and produce invalid actions.

---

## Transition to Generative Policies

Generative policies model action distributions instead of a single deterministic action:

$$
a_t \sim p_\theta(a_t \mid o_t)
$$

This motivates diffusion-based policies for high-dimensional, multimodal robot action generation.
